title: HTML-First Composition Strategies for Re-skinning an A11y-Complete Block
summary: Prior-art grounding for backlog #1795: the standards-based reuse menu WE should offer when designers bring many visual variations of one a11y-complete block (e.g. the nav-list block, W3C APG Disclosure Navigation), most of them mostly-visual-yet-not-pure-CSS — inject an icon, add a status badge, wrap a child in a popover, swap a sub-element. React's reuse menu (replace a sub-component, HOC/decoration, prop-merge, abstract-piece split, context config) maps onto five HTML-first, web-platform primitives, four of which already exist in the WE tree and most of which now ship natively: shadow <slot> (incl. imperative HTMLSlotElement.assign() / slotAssignment:'manual' for dynamic sub-component slotting); scoped custom element registries (now default in Chromium 146 + Safari) for sub-component replacement, the #854 CustomLink analog; CustomAttribute behaviors for child decoration (the HOC analog, e.g. RouteLinkBehavior); webinjectors IDREF + webexpressions {{ }} for context-driven configuration; and the abstract-piece-split convention (#023 'distinct tags', #715 tree-shakable traits). The survey finds these are composable, not mutually exclusive (support-all, per the fork-existence test) — so the live decision is the default seam-precedence for mostly-visual variation (compose-over-the-a11y-base vs. configure-one-block-to-render-variants, the #023 'distinct tags, not one configurable element' tension) and the forced invariant that every sanctioned strategy ship a conformance vector proving the re-skinned variant still passes the base block's a11y vectors (an a11y-composition suite that does not yet exist — the real build gap).
